storySettings.worldBackground: This is a world of imperium and furycraft at its zenith: the Aquilan Imperium, vast and absolute, ruled by the eternal Emperor Tiberius Corvus—the Old Raven—whose grip on power remains unshaken though succession looms uncertain. Furycraft—the commanding of numina, elemental fury-spirits that wake in a child and are mastered by will—runs in nearly every hand: most folk coax small workings from all six furies and true skill from one or two, and put them to daily labor (banking a hearth, turning a mill, steadying a cart) as readily as to war. But real FURYSTRENGTH—the power to command furies of might, to bond many of the Six, and above all to make them MANIFEST in visible animal shape—is rare, and it is bred and hoarded within the patrician bloodlines of the Twelve High Houses, each strong in several signature furies: Terra (stone and overwhelming strength), Ventus (wind and lightning and flight), Ignis (flame and passion and madness), Aqua (water and healing and the reading of hearts), Ferrum (metal and battle-prowess and shattering resistance), and Silva (wood and archery and the green-dark stealth). The Crown's House Corvus is ancient and masterful; the rival war-houses Drusus and Sabinus ward the barbarian North; the succession contenders Aurelius (golden, beloved) and Crassus (brutal schemer) circle the throne; the stabilizers Serenus and the fading Fabius (keepers of elder wards whose purpose none fully recall) hold the balance; the eastern Pontius commands frontier and siege-craft; the maritime admirals Ventidius and Murena wage endless naval war against Tharsis; and the lesser houses Varro and Tullius hunger for station. The law itself measures the gift: the Censorate tests every child's furies as they wake and enrolls the strong among the CITIZENRY, who alone hold full rights—and who are bound by the Lex Numinae to marry only their own in strength, that furycraft breed true. The rudimentary-crafting masses serve hearth and field and never taste those rights; and a wild-born commoner in whom great strength wakes outside the sanctioned lines is a scandal, a threat, a coveted prize, or a silent grave. The Collegium Furiacae—the Academy at the heart of Aquila—forges the Houses' strong-born young into rivals, lovers, and conspirators beneath the Emperor's distant, piercing eye. Across the Mare Internum, the Tharsi merchant-princes and blood-priests—masters of a sorcery born from sacrifice and god-hunger, wholly alien to Furycraft—press relentlessly, and the present war grinds upon a wealthy free port of the East that neither empire can afford to lose. Magic exacts its price: Vis, the reserve drawn from the gifted themselves, and the greatest feats—flight, true healing, the fury made flesh—demand a crafter strong enough to manifest and lavish Vis to do it. The Twelve Thrones—true gods who walk the world—scheme in service of no mortal; barbarians rage beyond the northern limes; eastern kingdoms flicker in perpetual twilight; the Senate endures as pageantry above hidden machinery. Yet beneath all this—sealed in vaults forgotten by all but the Keepers—something ancient and alien stirs, a hive-intelligence from before the world's making that devours the very numina, waiting only for the old wards to weaken. The real war is the one no one yet sees.
storySettings.narratorStyle: The prose is vivid, erudite, and steeped in the gravity of empire—Latin forms, ranks of iron, classical weight and dignity—yet grim and propulsive, never ornamental. Sentences are muscular and precise, favoring clarity over decoration. Magic is mythic and terrible: numina are rendered as tangible presences; Vis-expenditure drains the caster's reserves, leaving them drained and vulnerable if overextended; cost and choice are inseparable. Political intrigue crackles with real stakes: a whispered phrase births provinces of war; a House's disgrace echoes through generations of bloodline shame. Violence is common and brutal, yet the world is not bleak—cunning, loyalty, and audacity burn brilliantly against the darkness. Pacing favors momentum: scenes move swiftly; dialogue cuts clean and sharp; action escalates toward irrevocable consequence. The narrative embraces moral complexity: the Aquilani wage wars both just and necessary; the Tharsi blood-priests believe utterly in their gods; wild-born gift-bearers are neither helpless victims nor destined saviors. Emperors rule with iron will; the Twelve Thrones meddle with their own unfathomable agendas. Hints of the unknown—ancient seals, whispered wards, the stirring of old things in sealed places, warnings that mortals misread or ignore—drift beneath the surface, ominous but never fully revealed. The narrator is intelligent, responsive to player choice, and aware of the world's hidden machinery, even as it remains veiled from the characters themselves.
